does: This paper develops a comprehensive framework for video understanding reward modeling, introducing VURB, a benchmark of 2,100 preference pairs with detailed chain-of-thought reasoning traces averaging 1,143 tokens each, evaluated through majority voting across general, long, and reasoning-oriented tasks. The authors construct VUP-35K, a large-scale dataset of 35,000 preference pairs generated via a fully automated pipeline to provide high-quality supervision for training. They train two reward model variants—VideoDRM (discriminative) and VideoGRM (generative)—both achieving state-of-the-art results on VURB and VideoRewardBench. Analysis demonstrates that VUP-35K improves both reward prediction accuracy and the reasoning capabilities of video understanding models, addressing a critical gap in multimodal reward modeling where video has lagged substantially behind text and image domains.
connects: This work builds directly on recent advances in multimodal reward modeling across text and images, extending these successes to the video domain where robust benchmarks and preference data remain scarce. It responds to the broader shift toward learning from human preferences and learned reward models rather than hand-crafted metrics, following the trajectory of work in text reward models, image reward models, and preference-based learning for multimodal systems. The paper leverages established practices in benchmark construction with majority voting evaluation and chain-of-thought reasoning annotations, adapted from recent efforts in preference annotation and model evaluation. It also connects to work on generative versus discriminative approaches to reward modeling and the use of automated pipelines for data construction, building on techniques from synthetic data generation and preference data curation.
enables: This work opens new directions for improving AI systems through video preference feedback, enabling downstream applications in video generation quality assessment, long-horizon video understanding, and reasoning-intensive multimodal tasks. VURB and VUP-35K provide foundational resources for training increasingly sophisticated video reward models that can guide policy learning in video generation and editing systems. The framework's modular design allows extension to other video domains and tasks, and the automated data construction pipeline could accelerate preference dataset creation for new multimodal challenges. Future work can leverage these reward models to train better video generation models through reinforcement learning, develop more efficient reward model architectures for real-time video feedback, and investigate how chain-of-thought reasoning in rewards transfers to improved downstream model reasoning across long, complex video understanding tasks.
